title: RAP Managed Scenario — Deep Internals
description: How RAP managed scenario actually works under the hood — EML internals, early vs late numbering, determination execution order, validation semantics, feature control, draft handling, and authorization integration.

sections.title: Transactional Composition Internals

description.value:
RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) managed scenario implements a complete transactional domain-driven design framework on top of SAP's application server and HANA database. Understanding the internal architecture is essential for Staff+ engineers who need to troubleshoot performance issues, design complex business objects, or extend the framework's behavior.

Architecture Layers: (1) BDEF (Behavior Definition) — the declarative contract that defines the business object structure: which entities are part of the composition, which operations are allowed, and which framework features (numbering, determination, validation) are active. The BDEF is compiled into CCL (Core Component Language) metadata stored in the ABAP Repository. (2) Behavior Pool — the ABAP class that implements the behavior. In managed scenario, the framework provides default implementations for all standard operations (CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE), and you only implement custom logic (determinations, validations, actions). (3) RAP Runtime (SADL + EML) — the execution engine that interprets the BDEF metadata and orchestrates the transaction flow: numbering, determination execution, validation, feature control, and the final database commit.

Transactional Buffer: RAP uses a multi-level transactional buffer: (1) ABAP internal tables for the current interaction (draft or active). (2) Database tables for persistent storage. (3) The RAP buffer layer that tracks changes, maintains before/after images, and ensures transactional consistency. Understanding this buffer is key to debugging issues like stale data in determinations.

Staff+ Insight: RAP managed scenario is SAP's implementation of the Unit of Work pattern (Martin Fowler, Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture). The transactional buffer tracks all changes within a transaction, and the flush operation at COMMIT writes all changes atomically. This is identical to Hibernate's persistence context or JPA's EntityManager.

decisionTree.prompt: Should you use managed or unmanaged RAP for this business object?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "How much of the standard CRUD behavior matches your requirement?" If CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE is standard data persistence with simple validations, managed is correct. If you need custom locking, late numbering with complex business rules, or non-standard transaction control, unmanaged is required.

branches.condition: Standard CRUD with simple validations and determinations
branches.path: Managed scenario. ~50 lines of behavior pool code. Built-in draft, locking, numbering. Save ~80% boilerplate vs unmanaged. Use for: simple master data, standard document processing.

branches.condition: Complex create with legacy number ranges, cross-BO validation
branches.path: Unmanaged scenario. Manual CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE implementations. ~400+ lines per entity. Required for: integration scenarios, legacy BAPI wrappers.

branches.condition: Existing BAPI/function module needs RAP wrapper
branches.path: Unmanaged with BAPI call in create/update methods. This is the migration pattern: wrap existing function modules in RAP behavior, migrate to managed incrementally.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Use managed for 80% of entities. For the 20% that need custom logic, isolate it in a dedicated class and migrate to managed when the framework catches up.

sections.title: EML Deep Dive

description.value:
EML (Entity Manipulation Language) is the ABAP runtime API for interacting with RAP business objects. Understanding how EML statements compile and execute is essential for writing efficient code and debugging performance issues. EML is not a query language — it's a transactional command language designed specifically for RAP business object operations.

MODIFY Internals: When you write MODIFY ENTITIES OF Z_Travel, the following happens: (1) The EML compiler translates the ABAP statement into a parameterized call to the RAP runtime. (2) The runtime reads the BDEF metadata to determine the allowed operations, feature control, and authorization requirements. (3) The runtime creates transactional buffer entries (before images) for the affected entities. (4) The runtime executes the MODIFY in phases: pre-processing (check authorizations, check feature control), main processing (execute determinations, validate data), and post-processing (update buffer, trigger side effects). (5) The changes are NOT written to the database until SAVE or COMMIT.

READ Internals: READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel bypasses the transactional buffer and reads directly from the database (or the draft buffer if a draft exists). The READ statement respects DCL authorization — if the user lacks authorization for a record, it is invisible. READ can read by key, by association (ALL ENTITIES), or with explicit field selection.

EXECUTE Internals: EXECUTE runs a RAP action. Actions are the only RAP operation that can return a result. The execution flow: (1) Read the entity instance. (2) Check feature control (is the action enabled?). (3) Check authorization. (4) Execute the action method in the behavior pool. (5) Write any resulting changes to the transactional buffer.

Staff+ Insight: EML is similar to GraphQL mutations — you specify what you want to do and the fields you want returned, and the framework optimizes the execution. The key difference is that EML is transactional by default (all changes within a MODIFY block are part of the same LUW), while GraphQL mutations are typically independent.

"""
  EML Deep Dive Examples
"""

" MODIFY - Create with early numbering
MODIFY ENTITIES OF Z_Travel
  ENTITY Travel
  CREATE
    FROM @( VALUE #(
      ( %cid: 'T1'
        AgencyID = '000010'
        CustomerID = '000100'
        BeginDate = '20250101'
        EndDate = '20250115'
        %control-AgencyID = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        %control-CustomerID = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        %control-BeginDate = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        %control-EndDate = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) ) )
  MAPPED DATA(ls_mapped)
  REPORTED DATA(ls_reported)
  FAILED DATA(ls_failed).

" After MODIFY:
" - ls_mapped-travel contains %cid -> actual TravelID mapping
" - TravelID is set by 'setInitialStatus' determination

" READ - fetch by key with field selection
READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel
  ENTITY Travel
  FROM VALUE #( ( TravelID = '00001000' ) )
  RESULT DATA(lt_travel)
  REPORTED DATA(ls_reported_read)
  FAILED DATA(ls_failed_read).

" READ with association fetch
READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel
  ENTITY Travel
  FROM VALUE #( ( TravelID = '00001000' ) )
  RESULT DATA(lt_travel_with_assoc)
  ENTITY _Booking
  FROM VALUE #( ( TravelID = '00001000' ) )
  RESULT DATA(lt_bookings).

" EXECUTE - run action
EXECUTE
  ACTION Z_Travel~acceptTravel
  FROM VALUE #( ( TravelID = '00001000' ) )
  RESULT DATA(lt_action_result)
  REPORTED DATA(ls_reported_action)
  FAILED DATA(ls_failed_action).

" MODIFY with update and side effects
MODIFY ENTITIES OF Z_Travel
  ENTITY Travel
  UPDATE
    FROM VALUE #( ( TravelID = '00001000'
                    EndDate = '20250131'
                    %control-EndDate = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) )
  ENTITY Booking
  UPDATE
    FROM VALUE #( ( TravelID = '00001000'
                    BookingID = '00000001'
                    Status = 'C'
                    %control-Status = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) ).

sections.codeBlock:
" MODIFY with CREATE
MODIFY ENTITIES OF Z_Travel
  ENTITY Travel
  CREATE FROM @( VALUE #(
    ( %cid: 'T1'
      AgencyID = '000010'
      CustomerID = '000100'
      BeginDate = '20250101'
      EndDate = '20250115'
      %control-AgencyID = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
      %control-CustomerID = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
      %control-BeginDate = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
      %control-EndDate = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) ) )
  MAPPED DATA(ls_mapped)
  REPORTED DATA(ls_reported)
  FAILED DATA(ls_failed).

" READ by key
READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel
  ENTITY Travel
  FROM VALUE #( ( TravelID = '00001000' ) )
  RESULT DATA(lt_travel).

" EXECUTE action
EXECUTE
  ACTION Z_Travel~acceptTravel
  FROM VALUE #( ( TravelID = '00001000' ) )
  RESULT DATA(lt_action_result).

quantifiedTradeoff.title: EML Overhead Per Operation — Framework Cost vs Manual SQL

variables.name: Operation mix
variables.source: Fiori app telemetry for Travel BO

eml.monthlyPenalty: $1,800 — 500 users × 20 operations/min × 12ms × 730h = ~1,460 hours of framework overhead. However, this includes auth checks, buffering, and draft handling that you'd need to build manually.

manual_sql.monthlyPenalty: $450 — Pure SQL is faster per operation but you lose: draft management, automatic locking, authorization framework, and determination orchestration. Building these manually costs ~$40K in development.

calculation.breakeven: EML overhead pays for itself if your BO needs >3 of these features: draft, locking, auth, determinations, validations. At 500+ users, the framework overhead is ~9ms per call, but manual implementation of the same features would add 15-25ms of custom ABAP code.

sections.title: Early vs Late Numbering

description.value:
Numbering in RAP determines when entity keys are assigned — before or after the transactional buffer is populated. This seemingly small decision has profound implications for the sequence of events, the ability to use keys in other entities, and transactional buffer behavior.

Early Numbering: Keys are assigned during the MODIFY phase, before any determinations or validations run. This means the key is available immediately — you can use %pid (preliminary ID) in child entities to reference the parent, and the key appears in the mapped response. Early numbering is implemented via a numbering managed declaration in the BDEF plus a field ( numbering : managed ) annotation on the key field. The framework generates the key using a number range object (configured via NUMBERING transaction).

Late Numbering: Keys are assigned at SAVE time, after all determinations and validations have passed. This is useful when the key depends on business logic that must run first (e.g., a document number based on document type and fiscal year). The key is not available during the MODIFY phase — you must use %cid references in child entities. Late numbering requires explicit implementation in the behavior pool's late_numbering method.

Impact on Draft: Early numbering assigns the key at draft creation time — the draft record has the final key. Late numbering assigns the key at activation time — the draft record has a temporary key until activation. This affects how you reference records in draft state and how the OData service handles $expand on draft entities.

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 380" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.num-box{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:8}.num-ph{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--text-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:6}.num-lbl{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:system-ui;font-size:11px;font-weight:600;text-anchor:middle}.num-sub{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:system-ui;font-size:9px;text-anchor:middle}.num-flow{stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;fill:none;marker-end:url(#arrow)}.num-early{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:6}.num-late{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:#e67e22;stroke-width:2;rx:6}.ann-pulse{animation:pulse 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes pulse{0%,100%{opacity:.6}50%{opacity:1}}@keyframes flowDash{0%{stroke-dashoffset:-8}100%{stroke-dashoffset:0}}</style><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6 Z" fill="var(--brand-primary)"/></marker></defs><rect class="num-box" x="15" y="15" width="670" height="350"/><text class="num-lbl" x="350" y="42">Early vs Late Numbering — Sequence Comparison</text><rect class="num-early" x="30" y="65" width="290" height="45"/><text class="num-lbl" x="175" y="90">Early Numbering</text><text class="num-sub" x="175" y="105">Key assigned at MODIFY time</text><rect class="num-late" x="370" y="65" width="290" height="45"/><text class="num-lbl" x="515" y="90">Late Numbering</text><text class="num-sub" x="515" y="105">Key assigned at SAVE time</text><text class="num-lbl" x="175" y="145">Sequence:</text><text class="num-lbl" x="515" y="145">Sequence:</text><text class="num-sub" x="175" y="162">1. MODIFY entered</text><text class="num-sub" x="515" y="162">1. MODIFY entered</text><text class="num-sub" x="175" y="178">2. Key assigned (number range)</text><text class="num-sub" x="515" y="178">2. %cid used as placeholder</text><text class="num-sub" x="175" y="194">3. Determinations run (key available)</text><text class="num-sub" x="515" y="194">3. Determinations run (%cid only)</text><text class="num-sub" x="175" y="210">4. Validations run</text><text class="num-sub" x="515" y="210">4. Validations run</text><text class="num-sub" x="175" y="226">5. SAVE → commit to DB</text><text class="num-sub" x="515" y="226">5. SAVE → late_numbering method</text><text class="num-sub" x="175" y="242">→ Draft has final key</text><text class="num-sub" x="515" y="242">→ 6. Key assigned, commit to DB</text><text class="num-sub" x="515" y="258">→ Draft has temporary key</text><rect class="num-ph" x="80" y="275" width="540" height="55"/><text class="num-lbl" x="350" y="293">When to use which?</text><text class="num-sub" x="350" y="310">Early: Simple ID generation, children reference parent via %pid</text><text class="num-sub" x="350" y="325">Late: Key depends on business logic (doc type + fiscal year)</text></svg>

"""
  BDEF configuration for numbering
"""

" BDEF: managed numbering (early)
managed implementation in class zbp_rap_travel unique;
define behavior for Z_Travel alias Travel
{
  numbering managed;
  field ( numbering : managed ) TravelID;
}

" BDEF: late numbering (custom implementation)
managed implementation in class zbp_rap_document unique;
define behavior for Z_Document alias Document
{
  numbering late;
  field ( numbering : late ) DocumentID;
}

"""
  Behavior pool: Late numbering implementation
"""
CLASS zbp_rap_document IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD late_numbering FOR NUMBERING
    IMPORTING entities FOR CREATE Document.

    DATA: lv_number TYPE char10.

    LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_entity).
      DATA(lv_fiscal_year) =
        CONV char4( ls_entity-%data-CreationDate+0(4) ).

      lv_number = |{ ls_entity-%data-DocumentType }{ lv_fiscal_year }-|.
      CALL FUNCTION 'NUMBER_GET_NEXT'
        EXPORTING
          nr_range_nr = '01'
          object      = 'Z_DOC_NR'
        IMPORTING
          number       = DATA(lv_seq).

      lv_number = |{ lv_number }{ lv_seq WIDTH = 6 ALPHA = IN }|.

      SET KEY FROM ls_entity-%key
        TO ls_entity-%pid
        ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_key>).
      <fs_key>-DocumentID = lv_number.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I use early or late numbering for this business object?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the key depend on business logic that runs during the save sequence?" If the key can be assigned at MODIFY time (independent of other business data), use early numbering. If the key depends on fields set by determinations or on fiscal year context, use late numbering.

branches.condition: Simple sequential number, no dependency on other fields
branches.path: Early numbering. Key assigned at MODIFY time. Available immediately in mapped response. Child entities can reference through %pid. Draft record has final key from creation.

branches.condition: Key depends on document type, fiscal year, or determination output
branches.path: Late numbering. Key assigned at SAVE time. Use %cid references in child entities. Draft record has temporary key until activation.

branches.condition: OData service needs immediate key in create response
branches.path: Early numbering. OData create response includes the final key. Late numbering requires a separate READ after activation to get the key.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Early numbering is simpler and preferred unless you have an explicit dependency on save-time data. Late numbering adds complexity to draft handling and child entity references.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Early vs Late Numbering — Key Assignment Cost

early.monthlyPenalty: $0 — Early numbering is built into the framework. No additional implementation cost. Zero runtime overhead for key assignment.

late.monthlyPenalty: $7,200 — Late numbering: 3 extra methods × 2 days each = 6 dev days ($7,200). Runtime: 5ms per create × 200 creates/h × 730h = 0.2 hours/month extra CPU. Acceptable overhead but adds complexity.

calculation.breakeven: Early numbering is strictly cheaper on both dev cost and runtime. Only use late numbering when business rules require save-time key assignment (e.g., fiscal year + document type composite key).

sections.title: Determination Execution Order

description.value:
Determinations are one of the most powerful features of RAP managed scenario — they automatically execute business logic at specific trigger points. However, the execution order of multiple determinations is often misunderstood, leading to subtle bugs where one determination runs before another's data is ready.

Trigger Points: Determinations are defined in the BDEF with trigger conditions: determination <name> on modify { create; } — runs when an entity is created. on modify { create; update; } — runs on create and update. on save { create; update; } — runs at SAVE time (after all MODIFY operations, before the commit). The trigger point determines when in the transaction lifecycle the determination executes.

Execution Order Rules: Within the same trigger group (e.g., on modify), determinations execute in the order they are defined in the BDEF. However, there are important nuances: (1) Parent before child — if a determination changes the parent entity, the children's determinations may re-execute. (2) After-image dependency — the framework tracks which fields changed and only re-executes determinations that depend on those fields (the field constraint on determinations). (3) Early vs late numbering — if you use early numbering, determinations see the final key; if late numbering, they see only %cid references.

Multiple FAs (Field Assignments): A single determination can modify multiple fields. The framework tracks before/after images of every field. If Determination A modifies Field X, and Determination B has field X in its definition, B re-executes after A (potentially in a cycle). The framework detects cycles and prevents infinite loops.

"""
  BDEF: Multiple determinations with execution order
"""
define behavior for Z_Travel alias Travel
{
  " Execution order: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 (within same trigger)

  determination setInitialStatus on modify { create; }        " (1)
  determination setDefaultAgency on modify { create; }        " (2)
  determination setCurrencyFromOrg on modify { create; }      " (3)
  determination calculateTotalPrice on modify { create; update; } " (4)

  " These run at SAVE time, AFTER all modify determinations
  determination checkTravelPolicy on save { create; update; } " (5)
}

"""
  Behavior pool implementation
"""
CLASS zbp_rap_travel IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD determination_setInitialStatus FOR DETERMINATION
    ON MODIFY
    IMPORTING keys FOR Travel~setInitialStatus.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF Z_Travel IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY Travel
      UPDATE FROM VALUE #(
        FOR key IN keys (
          TravelID = key-TravelID
          Status   = 'O'  " Open
          %control-Status = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) )
      REPORTED DATA(ls_reported)
      FAILED DATA(ls_failed).

    reported-travel = VALUE #(
      FOR key IN keys (
        %tky = key-%tky
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
          severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-success
          text     = |Initial status set to 'Open'| ) ) ).
  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD determination_calculateTotalPrice FOR DETERMINATION
    ON MODIFY
    IMPORTING keys FOR Travel~calculateTotalPrice.

    READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY Travel
      FROM VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( TravelID = key-TravelID ) )
      RESULT DATA(lt_travel)
      ENTITY _Booking
      FROM VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( TravelID = key-TravelID ) )
      RESULT DATA(lt_bookings).

    LOOP AT lt_travel ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_travel>).
      DATA(lv_total) = REDUCE dec16(
        INIT x = 0
        FOR booking IN lt_bookings WHERE ( TravelID = <fs_travel>-TravelID )
        NEXT x = x + booking-FlightPrice ).

      MODIFY ENTITIES OF Z_Travel IN LOCAL MODE
        ENTITY Travel
        UPDATE FROM VALUE #(
          ( TravelID = <fs_travel>-TravelID
            TotalPrice = lv_total
            %control-TotalPrice = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) ).
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" BDEF: Execution order (1→2→3→4, then 5 at save)
define behavior for Z_Travel {
  determination setInitialStatus on modify { create; }         " (1)
  determination setDefaultAgency on modify { create; }         " (2)
  determination setCurrencyFromOrg on modify { create; }       " (3)
  determination calculateTotalPrice on modify { create; update; } " (4)
  determination checkTravelPolicy on save { create; update; } " (5)
}

" Behavior pool: setInitialStatus
METHOD determination_setInitialStatus FOR DETERMINATION
  ON MODIFY
  IMPORTING keys FOR Travel~setInitialStatus.
  MODIFY ENTITIES OF Z_Travel IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY Travel
    UPDATE FROM VALUE #(
      FOR key IN keys (
        TravelID = key-TravelID
        Status   = 'O'
        %control-Status = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) ).
ENDMETHOD.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "My determinations are firing in the wrong order. D4 calculates total price before D1 sets the status, so the price is always zero."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Re-order the determinations in the BDEF." (oversimplified, doesn't address the real issue)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "This is a classic determination ordering problem. Let me analyze it: Determinations within the same trigger group fire in BDEF definition order — D1 before D4. If D4 sees a zero price, the issue isn't ordering but dependency. D4 likely depends on a field that D1 hasn't set yet, OR D4 is reading from an entity that hasn't been fully populated. Three solutions: (1) Add a field-level constraint to D4 — specify that D4 depends on the status field set by D1. The framework then automatically re-executes D4 after D1 completes. (2) Use on save instead of on modify for D4 — the framework guarantees all on-modify determinations complete before any on-save determination runs. (3) If D4 must run on modify but depends on D1's output, implement an explicit READ ENTITIES in D4 to get the current state rather than relying on the passed keys. The root cause is usually that D4 receives the %tky before D1 has modified the entity — D4 should either use %control fields or explicitly read the updated state."
adversarialResponse.pattern: root-cause-analysis — identify the actual cause (dependency vs ordering), provide multiple solutions with tradeoffs, and explain the framework behavior (field-level constraints, trigger groups, before/after images).

sections.title: Validation Semantics

description.value:
Validations in RAP are the mechanism for enforcing business rules that must prevent data from being saved. Unlike determinations (which can modify data), validations are read-only checks — they can detect problems but cannot fix them. The validation framework ensures data integrity before the COMMIT is executed.

When They Fire: Validations defined with on save execute when the SAVE operation is triggered — either explicitly via the RAP SAVE or implicitly when the OData service calls the save sequence. Validations defined with on modify execute during the MODIFY phase, before the transactional buffer is committed. The choice depends on when you want the error to be raised: at save time (allows interactive correction) or at modify time (rejects immediately).

Failure Semantics: When a validation fails, the framework: (1) Stores the error in the FAILED table. (2) Does NOT commit the transaction. (3) Returns control to the caller with sy-subrc = 4. (4) The entire LUW is still active — the caller can fix the data and retry. The validation does NOT trigger a rollback — it simply prevents the COMMIT. This is critical for interactive UIs (Fiori) where the user should be able to correct errors.

Multiple Validations: If multiple validations are defined, ALL validations execute even if the first one fails. This gives the user a complete list of errors in one roundtrip, rather than fixing one error only to find another. The FAILED table accumulates errors from all validations. The commit only proceeds if FAILED is empty.

"""
  BDEF: Validation definitions
"""
define behavior for Z_Travel alias Travel
{
  validation validateCustomer on save { create; update; }
  validation validateDates on save { create; update; }
  validation validateAgency on modify { create; }
}

"""
  Behavior pool: Validation implementations
"""
CLASS zbp_rap_travel IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD validation_validateCustomer FOR VALIDATION
    ON SAVE
    IMPORTING keys FOR Travel~validateCustomer.

    READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY Travel
      FROM VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( TravelID = key-TravelID ) )
      RESULT DATA(lt_travel).

    LOOP AT lt_travel INTO DATA(ls_travel).
      SELECT SINGLE @abap_true FROM I_BusinessPartner
        WHERE BusinessPartner = @ls_travel-CustomerID
        INTO @DATA(lv_customer_exists).

      IF lv_customer_exists <> abap_true.
        APPEND VALUE #( %tky = ls_travel-%tky ) TO failed-travel.

        APPEND VALUE #(
          %tky = ls_travel-%tky
          %msg = new_message_with_text(
            severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
            text     = |Customer { ls_travel-CustomerID } does not exist| )
          %element-CustomerID = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        ) TO reported-travel.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD validation_validateDates FOR VALIDATION
    ON SAVE
    IMPORTING keys FOR Travel~validateDates.

    READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY Travel
      FROM VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( TravelID = key-TravelID ) )
      RESULT DATA(lt_travel).

    LOOP AT lt_travel INTO DATA(ls_travel).
      IF ls_travel-BeginDate > ls_travel-EndDate.
        APPEND VALUE #( %tky = ls_travel-%tky ) TO failed-travel.

        APPEND VALUE #(
          %tky = ls_travel-%tky
          %msg = new_message_with_text(
            severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
            text     = |Begin date must be before end date| )
          %element-BeginDate = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
          %element-EndDate   = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        ) TO reported-travel.
      ENDIF.

      IF ls_travel-BeginDate < sy-datum.
        APPEND VALUE #( %tky = ls_travel-%tky ) TO failed-travel.

        APPEND VALUE #(
          %tky = ls_travel-%tky
          %msg = new_message_with_text(
            severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-warning
            text     = |Begin date is in the past| )
          %element-BeginDate = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        ) TO reported-travel.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" BDEF: Validation definitions
define behavior for Z_Travel {
  validation validateCustomer on save { create; update; }
  validation validateDates on save { create; update; }
  validation validateAgency on modify { create; }
}

" Validation: validateCustomer
METHOD validation_validateCustomer FOR VALIDATION
  ON SAVE
  IMPORTING keys FOR Travel~validateCustomer.
  READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY Travel
    FROM VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( TravelID = key-TravelID ) )
    RESULT DATA(lt_travel).
  LOOP AT lt_travel INTO DATA(ls_travel).
    SELECT SINGLE @abap_true FROM I_BusinessPartner
      WHERE BusinessPartner = @ls_travel-CustomerID
      INTO @DATA(lv_exists).
    IF lv_exists <> abap_true.
      APPEND VALUE #( %tky = ls_travel-%tky ) TO failed-travel.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %tky = ls_travel-%tky
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
          severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
          text = |Customer { ls_travel-CustomerID } not found| )
        %element-CustomerID = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
      ) TO reported-travel.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our validations are running but not preventing the save. Data is committed even when validations fail."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You must have a bug in your validation implementation." (blames developer, no systematic debugging)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Let me trace the validation execution path. There are three common reasons validations fail to prevent saves: (1) The validation is defined as on modify but should be on save. on modify validations run during MODIFY phase — if the framework gets a SAVE before the next dialog step, the on-save validations may not execute. Check your BDEF: validation on save is the correct trigger for preventing commits. (2) Your validation is returning errors in reported but not populating the failed table. The framework only prevents the commit if failed is populated — reported messages are informational. Ensure you're APPENDING to failed-<entity> for each failing record. (3) There's an early save or finalize save method that bypasses validation. If you've implemented custom save methods, you may be calling COMMIT WORK before the validation framework runs. Check for explicit COMMIT WORK in your behavior pool or BAPI calls. The fix: ensure all validations are on save, populate both failed and reported, and never call COMMIT WORK outside of the framework's save sequence."
adversarialResponse.pattern: systematic-triage — list the specific failure modes (trigger type, failed table population, early commit) with precise ABAP API references, and provide a step-by-step debugging approach.

sections.title: Feature Control

description.value:
Feature control is RAP's mechanism for dynamic enablement or disabling of operations based on the current state of the business object instance. This replaces the old pattern of manually checking authorization and status in every UI action. Feature control is declarative in the BDEF and implemented in a single method in the behavior pool.

What Can Be Controlled: (1) Operations — CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE for each entity. These can be enabled/disabled globally (in the BDEF) or dynamically (via feature control method). (2) Actions — each action can be enabled/disabled independently. For example, acceptTravel is available only when status = 'Open', rejectTravel only when status = 'Open' or 'Accepted'. (3) Fields — individual fields can be made read-only based on state. For example, once a travel is accepted, the BeginDate cannot be changed.

Implementation Pattern: The BDEF has a determine action control or feature control declaration. The behavior pool implements the get_instance_features method. This method receives the entity instances and returns a structure with %features — a bitmask that tells the framework which operations are enabled. The framework calls this method: (1) when the entity is loaded into the UI (to render buttons/fields), (2) before any operation is executed (to verify the operation is still valid).

Staff+ Insight: Feature control implements the state pattern (Gang of Four design pattern) at the framework level. The entity's state (status field) determines which operations are available. This is the same pattern used by workflow engines, state machines in distributed systems, and business process management tools.

"""
  BDEF: Feature control declarations
"""
define behavior for Z_Travel alias Travel
{
  " Dynamic control for instance-level operations
  instance feature control;

  " Actions that need dynamic control
  action acceptTravel result [1] $self;
  action rejectTravel result [1] $self;
  action cancelTravel result [1] $self;

  " Dynamic field control
  field ( features : instance ) BeginDate, EndDate, Status;
}

"""
  Behavior pool: Feature control implementation
"""
CLASS zbp_rap_travel IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD get_instance_features FOR FEATURES
    IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_features FOR Travel
    RESULT result.

    READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY Travel
      FROM VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( TravelID = key-TravelID ) )
      RESULT DATA(lt_travel).

    result = VALUE #(
      FOR ls_travel IN lt_travel (
        %tky = ls_travel-%tky

        " %features is a bitmask: use if_abap_behv=>fc-... constants
        %features = COND #(
          WHEN ls_travel-Status = 'O'  " Open
          THEN %features-\+update
               OR %features-\+delete
               OR %features-\+action( acceptTravel )
               OR %features-\+action( rejectTravel )
               OR %features-\+action-acceptTravel
               OR %features-\+action-rejectTravel

          WHEN ls_travel-Status = 'A'  " Accepted
          THEN %features-\+action-\+rejectTravel
               OR %features-\+action-cancelTravel

          WHEN ls_travel-Status = 'X'  " Cancelled
          THEN %features-%no-operation  " Read-only

          WHEN ls_travel-Status = 'C'  " Completed
          THEN %features-%action-cancelTravel

          ELSE %features-%no-operation
        )

        " Dynamic field control: BeginDate read-only if not Open
        %field-BeginDate = SWITCH #(
          ls_travel-Status
          WHEN 'O' THEN if_abap_behv=>fc-fld-enabled
          ELSE if_abap_behv=>fc-fld-read_only )

        %field-EndDate = SWITCH #(
          ls_travel-Status
          WHEN 'O' THEN if_abap_behv=>fc-fld-enabled
          ELSE if_abap_behv=>fc-fld-read_only )
      ) ).

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" BDEF: Feature control declarations
define behavior for Z_Travel {
  instance feature control;
  action acceptTravel result [1] $self;
  action rejectTravel result [1] $self;
  field ( features : instance ) BeginDate, EndDate, Status;
}

" Feature control: dynamic enablement by state
METHOD get_instance_features FOR FEATURES
  IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_features FOR Travel
  RESULT result.
  READ ENTITIES OF Z_Travel IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY Travel
    FROM VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( TravelID = key-TravelID ) )
    RESULT DATA(lt_travel).
  result = VALUE #(
    FOR ls_travel IN lt_travel (
      %tky = ls_travel-%tky
      %features = COND #(
        WHEN ls_travel-Status = 'O'
        THEN %features-\+update OR %features-\+delete
             OR %features-\+action-acceptTravel
             OR %features-\+action-rejectTravel
        WHEN ls_travel-Status = 'A'
        THEN %features-\+action-rejectTravel
             OR %features-\+action-cancelTravel
        WHEN ls_travel-Status = 'X'
        THEN %features-%no-operation
        ELSE %features-%no-operation )
      %field-BeginDate = SWITCH #( ls_travel-Status
        WHEN 'O' THEN if_abap_behv=>fc-fld-enabled
        ELSE if_abap_behv=>fc-fld-read_only ) ) ).
ENDMETHOD.

decisionTree.prompt: How should I implement dynamic enablement of operations for this business object?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the availability of operations depend on the entity's state?" If an action should be enabled only when Status = 'Open', you need instance feature control. If the same operations are always available for all instances, you don't need dynamic control.

branches.condition: Operations depend on entity state (status-based enablement)
branches.path: Instance feature control. Implement get_instance_features in behavior pool. Returns %features bitmask with %features-\+update, %features-\+delete, %features-\+action-<name>.

branches.condition: Operations depend on user authorization only
branches.path: Global authorization control via BDEF authorization master (instance). Use get_instance_authorizations to check PFCG roles. Simpler than feature control if state independence holds.

branches.condition: Fields should be read-only in certain states
branches.path: Instance field control. Add field ( features : instance ) to BDEF. Return %field-<name> = if_abap_behv=>fc-fld-enabled or fc-fld-read_only in get_instance_features.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Feature control implements the State pattern at the framework level. The entity's status field determines which operations are available. This is the same pattern used in workflow engines and state machines.

sections.title: Draft Handling & Authorization

description.value:
Draft handling is one of the most complex parts of RAP managed scenario. The framework provides a complete draft lifecycle — create draft, edit draft, discard draft, activate draft — automatically. Understanding the draft state machine is critical for troubleshooting issues like lost changes, stale drafts, and incorrect draft-to-active transitions.

Draft Lifecycle: (1) Draft Creation — when the user clicks 'Create' in the Fiori UI, the framework creates a draft record in the draft database table. The draft has a unique DraftID, the entity's key (if early numbering) or a temporary ID (if late numbering), and a reference to the active record (if editing an existing record). (2) Draft Editing — all changes are persisted to the draft table. The framework auto-saves drafts periodically (configurable in the Fiori app configuration). This is critical for user experience — users can close the browser and return to find their draft intact. (3) Draft Activation — when the user clicks 'Save', the framework validates, runs determinations, and transfers the draft data to the active table. (4) Draft Discard — the draft record is deleted from the draft table. If the active record exists (edit scenario), it remains unchanged.

Authorization Integration: Drafts respect the same DCL rules as active records. When a user reads a draft, the authorization check runs against the draft's data. If the user had authorization when the draft was created but loses it before activation, the activation may fail. This is a known pattern — handle it with appropriate authorization re-check in the precheck or validate methods.

Draft Internal Tables: Behind the scenes, RAP maintains 4 internal tables per draft for the composition tree: header data, item data, control data (which fields have been modified), and %is_draft flags. Understanding these tables is essential for debugging complex draft scenarios.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Draft Lifecycle Overhead — Framework-Managed vs Custom Draft

managed_draft.monthlyPenalty: $500 — Framework-managed: 1,000 creates/h × 2ms + 6,000 auto-saves/h × 2ms = ~14ms/s of DB time. Draft tables in HANA use column-store with minimal overhead.

custom_draft.monthlyPenalty: $4,200 — Custom draft: manual INSERT/SELECT on draft tables. 1,000 creates/h × 15ms + 6,000 auto-saves/h × 15ms = ~105ms/s. Plus development cost of ~$24K for draft lifecycle implementation.

calculation.breakeven: Managed draft is 7x cheaper in runtime and effectively free in development cost. Custom draft only makes sense when you need non-standard behavior like pre-approval workflows or unique constraint enforcement across draft/active.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our draft handling is causing deadlocks under high concurrency. 100 users editing the same document type are blocking each other."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Increase the number of work processes." (band-aid, not solving the root cause)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Deadlocks under high concurrent draft editing point to a lock contention problem, not insufficient resources. Let me diagnose: (1) RAP managed scenario locks the root entity during draft editing. If 100 users edit different documents, there should be no contention — each document has its own lock. If they're editing the SAME document (e.g., a shared template), that's a design issue — consider optimistic locking via ETag instead of pessimistic ENQUEUE locks. (2) Check if the draft auto-save interval (default 5 seconds) is too aggressive. Each auto-save triggers a write lock. Reduce to 30 seconds for low-priority documents. (3) The most common cause: a secondary index on the draft table is causing lock escalation. HANA may escalate row-level locks to table-level locks if a single transaction touches too many rows. Add a dedicated draft timestamp index to reduce scan range. (4) For truly high-contention scenarios, implement a custom draft store using a dedicated HANA table with light-weight optimistic locking and application-level conflict resolution."
adversarialResponse.pattern: analyze-then-optimize — identify the specific lock contention pattern, propose tiered solutions (config changes, index optimization, architectural redesign), and show understanding of HANA lock escalation behavior.

details: Staff+ Insight: RAP managed scenario is SAP's answer to the transactional domain-driven design pattern. FAANG interviewers asking about "design a transactional system" will resonate with this — it's CQRS + event sourcing + unit of work pattern. Frame RAP discussions in DDD (Domain-Driven Design) terms: aggregates, repositories, domain events.
